# 2 Chronicles 28: A King Who Chose His Own Way

Ahaz inherited the throne at twenty and immediately turned away from the LORD. This was not a gradual drift. The text presents his rebellion as deliberate and comprehensive: cast images of Baals, child sacrifice in the Valley of Ben-hinnom, incense at high places, idols under green trees. What made this especially grave was the contrast—David, his father, had done what was right in the LORD's eyes. Ahaz knew the standard. He rejected it anyway. The chronicler is clear about the cause and effect: Ahaz walked away from God, and God delivered him into the hands of enemies. In a single day, 120,000 men of Judah fell. Two hundred thousand more were taken captive. His kingdom was invaded from three directions—Aram, Israel, Edom, and the Philistines. This was not random misfortune. This was the LORD's judgment, explicit and overwhelming.

Yet in the middle of this catastrophe, something unexpected happens. When the victorious Israelite army brings their Judean captives back to Samaria, the prophet Oded confronts them. He speaks what the text calls God's wrath, but his words appeal to mercy and justice. The Spirit moves certain Ephraimite leaders—four men named specifically—to oppose slavery of their kinsmen. They do not deny the guilt or the anger of the LORD. Instead, they recognize their own guilt and refuse to compound it. So they release the captives and clothe the naked, tend their wounds, feed them, and escort them home. These leaders understand something Ahaz does not: that the judgment of God is real, but so is the possibility of repentance and restraint. They choose to step back from the brink.

Ahaz, meanwhile, spirals deeper. Attacked from all sides, he calls on Assyria for help—looking to human power instead of repenting before God. When Assyria comes, it afflicts rather than strengthens him. Desperate, Ahaz then commits the ultimate betrayal: he raids the house of God itself, strips it of its sacred articles, and uses them to pay the king of Assyria. Still unhelped, he abandons the God of his fathers entirely and begins sacrificing to the gods of Damascus—the very gods whose nations had defeated him. The logic is inverted and absurd, yet it reveals the hardened human heart. Ahaz sees his enemies' gods as having power and chooses to serve them. He cuts up the articles of the LORD's house, shuts its doors, and fills Jerusalem with altars to false gods. By the end, his unfaithfulness is not reactive—it is aggressive, systematic, and total. He dies and is buried not in the tombs of kings but in the city, denied the honor his office should have granted. Yet his son Hezekiah will reign in his place—and the grace of God has not abandoned His people, even when their king has abandoned Him.

The weight of this chapter lies in the portrait of a man who made every wrong choice available to him. Ahaz knew the way of David. He rejected it. When judgment came, he did not repent—he sought power elsewhere, paid tribute to foreign gods, and finally worshipped them. The LORD allowed him to experience the full consequence of his rebellion. But the chronicler also shows us that in the same moment Ahaz was failing, other men—lesser officials with no crown—heard the voice of a prophet and chose restraint, mercy, and fidelity to the LORD. The point is not that Ahaz's story offers hope for his own repentance. It does not. Rather, it shows us that the LORD's purposes are not dependent on any single king's faithfulness, and that even in an era of judgment, those who fear God and listen to His prophets can still choose the right way.
